requirements: The core contributions of the paper 'Ragged Paged Attention: A High-Performance and Flexible LLM Inference Kernel for TPU' have been replicated.

sub_tasks.requirements: The environment and infrastructure necessary to develop and benchmark RPA on TPU have been set up.
Code has been implemented to install JAX with TPU support including the Pallas kernel programming extension, matching versions used by the reference implementation in tpu-inference.

verification.spec: Run `python -c "import jax, jax.experimental.pallas as pl; print(jax.__version__, pl.__name__)"` and verify both imports succeed without error. Additionally import `from jax.experimental.pallas import tpu as pltpu` to confirm TPU Pallas backend is available.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to make the Mosaic MLIR-based compiler backend available for Pallas TPU kernel lowering.

verification.spec: Compile a trivial Pallas TPU kernel (e.g., elementwise add via `pl.pallas_call`) and verify it executes without raising a Mosaic-related ImportError. The successful lowering through Mosaic confirms the backend is wired up.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to provision access to a TPU7x (Ironwood) chip with 2 TensorCores, 192 GB HBM, 7380 GB/s peak HBM bandwidth, and 2307 BF16 TFLOPs/s peak compute, matching the benchmarking platform in Table 3.

verification.spec: Run `python -c "import jax; devs=jax.devices(); assert any('TPU' in d.device_kind for d in devs); print(devs)"` and verify at least one TPU device is reported. Then query device properties (e.g., via `jax.devices()[0].device_kind` and `jax.lib.xla_bridge.get_backend().platform_version`) to confirm the chip is TPU v7 (Ironwood). Additionally check that `jax.devices()[0].memory_stats()` reports HBM ~192 GB. Fail if no TPU v7 device is visible.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to install the vllm-project/tpu-inference package from the reference repository, so the RPA kernel under tpu_inference/kernels/ragged_paged_attention/v3/kernel.py can be imported.

verification.spec: Run `python -c "import importlib; m=importlib.import_module('tpu_inference.kernels.ragged_paged_attention.v3.kernel'); print(m.__file__)"` and verify the module loads without ImportError, confirming the tpu-inference package exposing the RPA v3 kernel is installed.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to install vLLM with the TPU backend such that RPA can be used as the attention kernel for end-to-end serving (vLLM-TPU integration).

verification.spec: Run `python -c "import vllm; from vllm.platforms import current_platform; print(vllm.__version__, current_platform.device_type)"` and verify the platform reports 'tpu'. Additionally confirm that vLLM's TPU attention backend module can be imported (e.g., `from vllm.attention.backends import tpu_attn` or the equivalent path in the installed version).

sub_tasks.requirements: Models and model configurations used in the paper have been acquired.
Code has been implemented to obtain the Llama 3 8B model configuration (32 Q heads, 8 KV heads, head_dim=128, BF16 precision) used across all benchmarks.

verification.spec: Load the Llama-3 8B config (e.g., via `transformers.AutoConfig.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B')`) and assert `num_attention_heads==32`, `num_key_value_heads==8`, `hidden_size//num_attention_heads==128`, and `torch_dtype` resolves to bfloat16. Fail otherwise.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to construct an alternate Llama-3-8B-like configuration with head_dim=256 for the dk=256 ablations reported in Figures 12, 13, 15 and Tables 5, 7, 9.

verification.spec: Import the benchmark configuration module and assert that a Llama-3-8B-like config variant exists with head_dim=256, num_query_heads=32, num_kv_heads=8, dtype=bf16. Verify via attribute access (e.g., cfg.head_dim == 256) that this alternate config is selectable for the dk=256 ablation sweeps.

sub_tasks.requirements: The Ragged Paged Attention kernel and all its core components have been implemented in Pallas.
The RPA preprocessing stage (Section 3.1) has been implemented correctly.

Code has been implemented so that Q, K, and V tensors carry an explicit packing dimension as their second-minor axis, forcing XLA to choose the minimum tile T(packing, 128) and avoiding implicit padding when hkv < packing.

verification.spec: Construct Q, K, V tensors and pass them through the RPA preprocessing step. Inspect their shapes and assert the second-minor axis equals the packing factor (e.g., pq=2 for BF16, pq=4 for INT8/FP8). Then trace the HLO via `jax.jit(fn).lower(...).compiler_ir('hlo')` for a small hkv=1 BF16 case and verify the tile assigned to Q/K/V is `T(packing, 128)` (i.e., T(2,128) for BF16), not the default T(8,128). The test passes if both the logical shape has packing as the second-minor dim AND no implicit padding to 8 rows is observed.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that the ragged token-count dimension s is placed in a non-tiled (non last-two) axis, enabling arbitrary dynamic slicing without unpack/blend/repack on VREGs.

verification.spec: Inspect the RPA kernel input layout: verify Q, K, V tensors are shaped such that the ragged token dimension s appears at a leading (non last-two) axis (e.g., Q shape (hkv, s, ceil(hg/pq), pq, dk)), not in the last two tiled dimensions.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to reshape Q from (s, hq, dk) into (hkv, s, ceil(hg/pq), pq, dk) so that hkv is the leading loop dimension and multiple query heads of a GQA group can be processed against the same Ck and Cv.

verification.spec: Inspect the Q preprocessing code path: verify Q is reshaped/transposed from (s, hq, dk) to (hkv, s, ceil(hg/pq), pq, dk) before kernel invocation, placing hkv as leading dim.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to interleave-pack K and V into a merged KV cache with shape (skv, ceil(2*hkv/pkv), pkv, dk) such that any single-token slice contains both K and V for all KV heads (Figure 7).

verification.spec: Allocate K and V tensors of shape (skv=4, hkv=2, dk=128) in BF16 with distinguishable values, run the KV-merge preprocessing, and verify the resulting merged KV cache has shape (skv, ceil(2*hkv/pkv), pkv, dk). Slice a single token (skv index t) from the merged cache and assert it contains both the K and V data for ALL hkv heads at position t (compare element-wise to the original K[t,:,:] and V[t,:,:]). The test fails if any K or V entry is missing from a single-token slice.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to load merged Ckv from VREGs and split it into Ck and Cv by unpacking and repacking, including the FP8 (8-bit) case where a single 32-bit word contains two merged KV elements per hkv.

verification.spec: Run RPA kernel with BF16 KV (pkv=2) and verify outputs match a reference attention implementation. Then run with FP8 KV (pkv=4) and verify the Ck/Cv separation code path handles extracting two 8-bit elements per 32-bit word: outputs must match a reference dequantized attention to within numerical tolerance.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to perform the Q/KV preprocessing (transpose, reshape, KV merge) at runtime inside the RPA pipeline rather than offline, matching Section 3.1.4.

verification.spec: Inspect the RPA pipeline entrypoint: verify Q transpose/reshape and K/V interleave-merge are performed at runtime (inside the JAX call surrounding the Pallas kernel) rather than expected as offline weight rewrites.

sub_tasks.requirements: The KV transpose fusion in the data loading path (Section 3.2) has been implemented.
Code has been implemented to fetch a KV block of shape (bkv, ceil(2*hkv/pkv), pkv, dk) from HBM to VMEM and reinterpret/shapecast it to a logical view of shape (bkv*2*hkv, dk).

verification.spec: Inspect kernel body: verify KV is fetched from HBM into VMEM with shape (bkv, ceil(2*hkv/pkv), pkv, dk) and then reinterpreted via shapecast/bitcast to a logical view of shape (bkv*2*hkv, dk) before strided loads.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to perform strided vector loads of shape (ckv, dk) with stride 2*hkv from VMEM into VREGs, materializing the KV transpose on-the-fly without an explicit transpose op.

verification.spec: Inspect the RPA kernel source (or HLO/MLIR of the lowered kernel) to confirm that KV loading from Bkv into VREGs uses a strided vector load with stride=2*hkv and load shape (ckv, dk), instead of an explicit transpose op. Concretely: grep the kernel module for `strided_load` / `pltpu.strided_load` (or equivalent shapecast + strided access) on the Bkv ref, and assert at least one such call uses stride proportional to 2*hkv. Also verify there is no `jnp.transpose` over the hkv axis in the inner compute loop.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to insert a small offset in the KV block layout in VMEM so that the effective stride during vector loads is an odd number, avoiding VMEM bank conflicts.

verification.spec: Read the RPA kernel source and assert that the KV block VMEM allocation includes a small offset/pad such that the effective stride (2*hkv adjusted) used in strided vector loads is odd. Concretely: locate the Bkv VMEM scratch allocation and confirm an explicit `+1` (or odd-stride padding constant) is applied to the leading dimension, with an accompanying comment or variable name referring to bank-conflict avoidance.

sub_tasks.requirements: The distribution-aware compilation strategy (Section 3.4) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented to pass dynamic segmentation indices [i, j, k] to the kernel, partitioning sequences into decode-only [0, i-1], fixed-size-chunk prefill [i, j-1], and mixed-batch [j, k-1] ranges.

verification.spec: Inspect kernel signature: verify three dynamic segmentation indices (i, j, k) are passed in and used to delineate decode-only [0,i-1], prefill-only [i,j-1], and mixed [j,k-1] sequence ranges with distinct dispatch behavior.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to dispatch a specialized compiled kernel per case enum (decode-only, prefill-only, mixed) with bq known at compile time in the first two cases.

verification.spec: Invoke the RPA entry point with three distribution configurations: (i) decode-only (all sequences with q_len=1), (ii) prefill-only with fixed-chunk q_len, (iii) mixed. Assert that the kernel receives/derives a `case` enum (or equivalent), and that the JIT cache contains three distinct compiled kernels (verify via `jax.jit` cache size or by tracing the lowered HLO and observing different bq constants baked in for cases (i) and (ii)). Test fails if all three workloads dispatch to the same compiled function.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for a post-scheduling reordering algorithm that places decode-only and prefill-only requests at the front of the batch before invoking the kernel.

verification.spec: Submit a batch of mixed sequences (decode, fixed-chunk prefill, mixed-length) to the scheduler/reordering routine; verify the returned ordering places all decode-only requests first, then prefill-only, then mixed, with index boundaries [i,j,k] computed correctly.

sub_tasks.requirements: The block-size tuning API (Section 3.5) has been implemented.
Code has been implemented to expose four tunable block-size parameters (bq, bkv, cq, ckv) at the kernel entry point.

verification.spec: Inspect the RPA kernel public entry point signature (e.g., `ragged_paged_attention(...)` in tpu_inference/kernels/ragged_paged_attention/v3/kernel.py) and assert that it accepts the four block-size parameters `bq`, `bkv`, `cq`, `ckv` (or an equivalent named tuple/dict). Use `inspect.signature` and assert all four names appear as parameters. Also verify the kernel accepts distinct block-size sets for decode/prefill/mixed (Section 3.5).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to accept three distinct block-size sets (decode-only, prefill-only with fixed-size chunked prefill, and mixed) and select among them based on the case enum at dispatch time.

verification.spec: Invoke the RPA dispatch with case=DECODE, case=PREFILL, case=MIXED; confirm three distinct (bq, bkv, cq, ckv) tuples are picked up (either via a config dict or compiled variants) and used by the corresponding kernel call.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented for an offline tuning utility that searches over (bq, bkv, cq, ckv) and writes the optimum into a lookup table consumed at server bring-up.

verification.spec: Run the offline tuning utility on a small (bq, bkv, cq, ckv) search grid for a fixed workload; verify it produces a lookup table (e.g., JSON/dict) keyed by workload signature and that the RPA dispatch consumes it at server start.

sub_tasks.requirements: Recompilation-prevention contract (Section 3.6) has been implemented.

Code has been implemented so that callers must specify a maximum number of tokens s and a maximum number of sequences n at server bring-up, and inputs are padded to these bounds so JIT shapes are static.

verification.spec: Call the RPA entry point twice: first with (s=2048, n=128) and a small batch padded to those bounds, then with (s=2048, n=128) and a different but smaller batch (also padded to s,n). Use `jax.jit` cache introspection (or measure compile time on the second call) to verify NO recompilation occurs. Then invoke a third time with s=4096 and verify that recompilation DOES occur. Also confirm the kernel raises or errors gracefully if inputs exceed s or n.

sub_tasks.requirements: Per-tensor quantization support (Section 3.7) has been implemented.
Code has been implemented to accept per-tensor scale factors for Q, K, and V and apply them inside the attention computation.

verification.spec: Construct quantized Q/K/V tensors (e.g., INT8 or FP8) along with per-tensor scalar scales sQ, sK, sV. Run the RPA kernel with these scales and compare the output against a reference BF16 attention computed on dequantized Q/K/V (Q*sQ, K*sK, V*sV). Assert max absolute element-wise error is within a quantization-appropriate tolerance (e.g., 5e-2 relative for FP8). Also verify the kernel signature accepts the three scalar arguments. Test fails if scales are ignored (e.g., output matches the un-scaled attention).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to automatically insert hkv padding when hkv per device is smaller than the packing factor, so tiling constraints are satisfied for quantized inputs.

verification.spec: Invoke RPA with quantized inputs configured such that hkv per device < packing factor (e.g., FP8 with hkv=1, pkv=4); verify preprocessing automatically pads hkv up to satisfy T(packing,128) tiling and the kernel runs and produces correct outputs.

sub_tasks.requirements: RPA attention-variant and TPU-generation coverage have been implemented.
Code has been implemented so that RPA correctly handles MHA, MQA, and GQA attention variants with arbitrary hq and hkv.

verification.spec: Run RPA on three configurations: MHA (hq=hkv), MQA (hkv=1), and GQA (hq=8, hkv=2) with arbitrary head counts; verify each invocation produces outputs matching a reference attention implementation within numerical tolerance.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented so that RPA can run on TPU v4 through v7 with only block-size retuning required between generations.

verification.spec: Inspect the RPA kernel source (tpu_inference/kernels/ragged_paged_attention/v3/kernel.py) and verify that TPU-generation-specific logic is parameterized only through block-size tuning parameters (bq, bkv, cq, ckv) and not through hardcoded generation-specific branches; verify the kernel exposes block sizes as configurable arguments and that the tuning lookup table accepts entries keyed by TPU generation (v4-v7).

sub_tasks.requirements: Benchmarking and evaluation infrastructure for Section 4 has been implemented.

Code has been implemented to construct decode-only benchmark inputs with n=128 sequences per RPA invocation, all assigned the same context length, scanned over context lengths {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768} as in Tables 4 and 5.

verification.spec: Import the benchmark input construction utility and call it for the decode-only regime. Assert that the produced inputs have exactly n=128 sequences per RPA invocation, that all sequences share the same context length, and that the context length sweep covers exactly {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768}. Verify by iterating each context length: returned input should have query length 1 per sequence and KV length equal to the swept context length for all 128 sequences.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to construct prefill-only benchmark inputs with n=1 sequence per RPA invocation, scanned over sequence lengths {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768} with and without causal masking, as in Tables 6-9.

verification.spec: Import the prefill benchmark input construction utility and invoke it for both causal=True and causal=False settings. Assert n=1 sequence per invocation and that the sequence length sweep equals {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768}. Verify that for each length s, the constructed input has exactly one sequence of that length (Q length = KV length = s), and that both causal masking modes are exercised.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute effective decode throughput (GB/s) using the formula in Section 4.1.1: ((context_length + 1) * 2*hkv*dk + 2*hq*dk) * bytes_per_element * batch / latency.

verification.spec: Call the throughput utility with known inputs (e.g., context_length=8192, hkv=8, hq=32, dk=128, bytes_per_element=2, batch=128, latency=1e-3 s) and assert the returned GB/s equals ((8192+1)*2*8*128 + 2*32*128) * 2 * 128 / 1e-3 / 1e9 within 1% relative error.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute MBU as Throughput / 7380 GB/s * 100% for the TPU7x peak HBM bandwidth used in the paper.

verification.spec: Call the MBU utility with throughput=6241 GB/s and assert it returns ~84.57% (within 0.1 absolute); call with throughput=7380 GB/s and assert it returns 100%. Verify the peak bandwidth constant 7380 is sourced from a named TPU7x spec constant in the code.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to compute prefill MFU as Speed / (2307 TFLOPs/s * max_MXU_utilization) * 100%, using 50% max MXU utilization for dk=128 and 100% for dk=256.

verification.spec: Call the MFU utility with speed=1000 TFLOPs/s, dk=128 and assert result equals 1000/(2307*0.5)*100 ≈ 86.7%. Call with dk=256 and assert result equals 1000/2307*100 ≈ 43.3%. Verify the max-MXU-utilization branch on dk is implemented (50% vs 100%).

sub_tasks.requirements: The decode-only experimental results from Section 4.1 and Tables 4-5 have been reproduced.
The reported peak decode MBU on Llama 3 8B reaches approximately 86% (paper_value=86, paper_unit='%', metric=peak decode MBU, tolerance allows verification of the claim in the abstract).

verification.spec: Run the RPA kernel in decode-only mode on Llama 3 8B (32 Q heads, 8 KV heads, dk=128, BF16) on a TPU7x Ironwood chip with n=128 sequences per RPA invocation and uniform KV context length sufficient to saturate HBM (>=8K tokens, e.g., 16K or 32K). Measure end-to-end RPA latency (including KV cache update + attention forward pass). Compute effective throughput = ((context_length * 2*hkv + 1*2*hkv + 2*hq) * dk * bytes_per_elem * n) / latency, then MBU = throughput / 7380 GB/s * 100%. Report the peak MBU across context lengths in [8K, 16K, 32K]; expected ~86%.

sub_tasks.requirements: At context length 8192, decode (dk=128, excl. prep) achieves throughput ~6241 GB/s and MBU ~84.57% as in Table 4.

verification.spec: Run the decode benchmark at context_length=8192, dk=128, n=128, and extract the 'excl_prep' MBU from the results table. Assert it is approximately 84.57% (within ±5 percentage points relative tolerance) and that the corresponding effective throughput is approximately 6241 GB/s.

sub_tasks.requirements: At context length 32768, decode (dk=128, excl. prep) achieves throughput ~6332 GB/s and MBU ~85.80% as in Table 4.

verification.spec: Run the decode-only RPA benchmark on TPU7x with Llama 3 8B config (dk=128, BF16), n=128, ctx=32768, excluding preprocessing. Compute MBU as in Section 4.1.1. Pass if measured MBU is within 5pp of 85.80%.

sub_tasks.requirements: At context length 4096, decode (dk=256, excl. prep) achieves throughput ~6274 GB/s and MBU ~85.02% as in Table 5.

verification.spec: Run the decode-only RPA benchmark on TPU7x with the dk=256 head-dim variant of Llama 3 8B (32 Q heads, 8 KV heads, dk=256, BF16), n=128, ctx=4096, excluding preprocessing. Compute MBU per Section 4.1.1. Pass if MBU within 5pp of 85.02%.

sub_tasks.requirements: At context length 8192, decode (dk=256, excl. prep) achieves MBU ~86.39% as in Table 5.

verification.spec: Run the decode-only RPA benchmark on TPU7x with the dk=256 head-dim variant (32 Q heads, 8 KV heads, dk=256, BF16), n=128, ctx=8192, excluding preprocessing. Compute MBU per Section 4.1.1. Pass if MBU within 5pp of 86.39%.

sub_tasks.requirements: The reproduced decode results show that HBM saturation is reached around 8K context length for dk=128 and around 4K for dk=256, matching Section 4.1.2.

verification.spec: Run the RPA decode benchmark on Llama 3 8B (32 Q heads, 8 KV heads, BF16) on TPU7x Ironwood with n=128 sequences, sweeping context length over {512, 1K, 2K, 4K, 8K, 16K, 32K} for both dk=128 and dk=256. For each configuration, compute MBU = effective_throughput / 7380 GB/s. Verify that MBU reaches >=85% by context length 8K for dk=128 and by context length 4K for dk=256, indicating HBM saturation. The reported peak MBU should be ~86%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Decode ablation results show that for context length >= 8K, the 'w/o FA' (DMA-only) latency closely matches the full kernel latency, confirming the memory-bound regime.

verification.spec: Run the decode ablation on Llama 3 8B on TPU7x with n=128 sequences and context lengths {512, 1K, 2K, 4K, 8K, 16K, 32K}. Measure latency for (a) full RPA kernel and (b) RPA with FlashAttention disabled ('w/o FA', DMA-only). For context length >= 8K, compute ratio = latency(w/o FA) / latency(full RPA). Verify this ratio is ~1.0 (within 0.2), confirming the kernel is memory-bound once HBM is saturated.

sub_tasks.requirements: The prefill experimental results from Section 4.2 and Tables 6-9 have been reproduced.
All non-causal prefill configurations across sequence lengths {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768} have been executed for dk=128 with n=1 and page_size=256.

verification.spec: Run the RPA prefill benchmark on TPU v7 (Ironwood) with causal=False, dk=128, n=1, page_size=256, hq=32, hkv=8, BF16, for each sequence length in {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768}. Assert each configuration runs to completion without error and produces a non-NaN output tensor with the expected shape (s, hq, dk). Record measured latency for each length so downstream MFU/throughput leaves can consume it.

sub_tasks.requirements: All non-causal prefill configurations across sequence lengths {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768} have been executed for dk=256 with n=1.

verification.spec: Execute the non-causal prefill benchmark for RPA on Llama 3 8B-like config with dk=256, n=1, causal=False, sweeping sequence lengths over the exact set {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768}. Verify benchmark completes for all 7 sequence lengths and outputs latency/throughput per length.

sub_tasks.requirements: All causal prefill configurations across sequence lengths {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768} have been executed for dk=128 with n=1.

verification.spec: Execute the causal prefill benchmark for RPA on Llama 3 8B on TPU7x with n=1, dk=128, causal=True, sweeping sequence lengths over the exact set {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768}. Verify benchmark completes for all 7 sequence lengths and reports per-length latency/throughput.

sub_tasks.requirements: All causal prefill configurations across sequence lengths {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768} have been executed for dk=256 with n=1.

verification.spec: Execute the causal prefill benchmark for RPA with dk=256, n=1, causal=True, sweeping sequence lengths over the exact set {512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768}. Verify benchmark completes for all 7 sequence lengths and produces per-length latency/throughput output.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced non-causal prefill (dk=128, excl. prep) reaches ~853 TFLOPs/s and ~73.98% MFU at sequence length 32768 (Table 6).

verification.spec: Run non-causal prefill on Llama 3 8B on TPU7x with n=1, dk=128, causal=False, sequence length s=32768. Measure the RPA kernel latency excluding preprocessing. Compute Speed = 4 * s^2 * h_q * d_k / latency (TFLOPs/s) and MFU = Speed / (2307 * 0.5) * 100%. Verify MFU is approximately 73.98% (within tolerance).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced non-causal prefill (dk=128, excl. prep) reaches ~73% MFU at sequence length 8192 (paper_value=72.88, paper_unit='%'), confirming the abstract's 73% prefill MFU claim.

verification.spec: Run the RPA kernel in prefill-only mode with a single sequence (n=1) of length s=8192 on Llama 3 8B configuration (32 Q heads, 8 KV heads, dk=128, BF16, no causal mask) on a TPU7x Ironwood chip. Measure attention forward + KV update latency excluding preprocessing. Compute Speed = (4 * s^2 * hq * dk) / latency in TFLOPs/s (non-causal). Compute MFU = Speed / (2307 TFLOPs/s * 0.5) * 100%, where 0.5 is the max MXU utilization for dk=128. Expected MFU ~= 73% (paper value 72.88%).

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced causal prefill (dk=128, excl. prep) reaches ~744 TFLOPs/s and ~64.52% MFU at sequence length 32768 (Table 8), supporting the paper's 63% causal MFU claim around saturation.

verification.spec: Run the prefill-only RPA benchmark on TPU7x with Llama 3 8B config (dk=128, BF16), causal=True, n=1, seq_len=32768, excluding preprocessing. Compute Speed using the causal-FLOPs definition in Section 4.2.1 (FLOPs include masked KV-block matmuls), then MFU = Speed / (2307 TFLOPs/s * 0.5). Pass if MFU within 5pp of 64.52%.
verification.paper_cite: Table 8 (prefill causal dk=128, seq=32768, excl. prep)

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced non-causal prefill (dk=256, excl. prep) reaches ~1674 TFLOPs/s and ~72.57% MFU at sequence length 32768 (Table 7), demonstrating roughly 2x FLOPs/s vs dk=128.

verification.spec: Run RPA prefill benchmark with Llama-3-8B-like config but dk=256, single sequence n=1, seq_len=32768, BF16, causal=False. Measure attention kernel runtime (excluding preprocessing) and compute MFU = Speed / (peak_BF16_TFLOPs * max_MXU_util). Check MFU within tolerance of 72.57%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced causal prefill (dk=256, excl. prep) reaches ~1416 TFLOPs/s and ~61.40% MFU at sequence length 32768 (Table 9).

verification.spec: Run RPA prefill benchmark with Llama-3-8B-like config but dk=256, n=1, seq_len=32768, BF16, causal=True. Measure attention kernel runtime excluding preprocessing and compute MFU. Verify within tolerance of 61.40%.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduced dk=256 prefill curve exhibits the inflection point near sequence length 8K (Figure 15) where attention computation overtakes preprocessing as the dominant cost.

verification.spec: From the dk=256 prefill runs, compute the ratio of total runtime including preprocessing at s=4096 to that at s=8192. The paper claims the curve decelerates near 8K. Verify (latency_incl_prep@4096 / s=4096) > (latency_incl_prep@8192 / s=8192) i.e. per-token cost decreases past 8K. Report the per-token cost ratio.

sub_tasks.requirements: Causal prefill ablation (Table 12, Figure 15) has been reproduced and shows similar overlap behavior with KV-update latency hidden by attention compute.

sub_tasks.requirements: Reproduce paper's reported baselines

Then run the same workload with RPA enabled; assert throughput_ratio = rpa_tokput / baseline_tokput is within tolerance of paper_value=3.5 (midpoint of 2x-5x range).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that a standard JAX/XLA scatter-based KV cache update baseline (KV update separated from attention, not fused) is implemented and benchmarked against RPA's fused-update kernel on Llama 3 8B decode on TPU7x.

verification.spec: Implement an unfused baseline that performs (1) a JAX/XLA scatter to update paged KV cache, then (2) a separate attention kernel, on TPU7x with Llama 3 8B decode, n=128 seqs, context=8K. Measure total latency of baseline (t_unfused) vs RPA fused (t_rpa). Assert t_unfused / t_rpa >= paper_value=1.2 (RPA at least matches and ideally improves over unfused).
verification.paper_cite: Section 1, 'eliminating TensorCore scatter overheads commonly incurred in today's JAX/XLA path'

verification.spec: Run RPA decode (n=128) on Llama 3 8B (dk=128, BF16) at TPU7x across context lengths {1K,2K,4K,8K,16K,32K} with KV update disabled. At ctx=8K, assert ratio (t_full - t_no_kv_update)/t_full is within tolerance of paper_value=0.05 (KV update overhead small/negligible per Figure 11).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that in prefill at seq_len >= 8K the compute-only ablation curve converges with the full RPA kernel curve, demonstrating KV update latency is fully hidden.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that an ablation comparing KV-transpose-fusion with strided odd-stride loads vs. naive strided loads (with bank conflicts) shows the odd-stride variant achieves lower latency on Llama 3 8B decode at TPU7x.

verification.spec: Implement two variants of the KV transpose fusion: (a) naive stride 2*h_kv that triggers VMEM bank conflicts; (b) odd-stride with small offset. Benchmark both on Llama 3 8B decode (n=128, ctx=8K) on TPU7x and assert t_naive / t_odd_stride >= paper_value=1.1.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that an ablation comparing merged interleaved KV layout vs. separate K and V layouts demonstrates lower DMA latency / fewer load-store ops on Llama 3 8B decode at TPU7x.

verification.spec: Run Llama 3 8B decode (n=128, ctx=8K, BF16, h_kv=1 per device via TP sharding) on TPU7x with two RPA variants: (a) separate K and V caches; (b) merged KV layout. Assert t_separate / t_merged >= paper_value=1.15 (merged is faster due to ~halved load/store ops and larger effective DMA).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that an ablation comparing distribution-aware compilation (specialized decode/prefill/mixed kernels dispatched per segment) vs. a single generic mixed-batch kernel shows lower latency on a representative mixed batch with Llama 3 8B / TPU7x.

verification.spec: Construct a mixed batch with i decode-only seqs, (j-i) fixed-chunk prefill seqs, and (k-j) mixed seqs (per Section 3.4). Run two configurations on Llama 3 8B / TPU7x: (a) one generic kernel for all, (b) distribution-aware dispatch using [i,j,k]. Assert t_generic / t_dispatch >= paper_value=1.2.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the online-softmax rescaling step updates running max m, denominator l, and output accumulator Co via exp(m_old - m_new) according to FlashAttention-2.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the P @ Cv accumulation correctly updates Co with the rescaled previous accumulator plus the new partial output.

verification.spec: For random P (cq x ckv) and Cv (ckv x dk), compute Co_new = scale * Co_old + P @ Cv via the implementation and assert match against jnp.einsum('qk,kd->qd', P, Cv) + scale*Co_old within atol=1e-2.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the kernel prefetches the next KV block (r'', j'') via async_copy and waits on its semaphore before consumption, achieving overlap with compute.

verification.spec: Inspect the kernel source for an async_copy issued for Bkv at next-iteration indices before the compute of the current iteration, and a corresponding semaphore_wait prior to use; additionally, profile and assert that KV-fetch DMA time overlaps with FA compute (DMA window starts before compute window ends) for a representative prefill run.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that newly projected KV entries Ukv are asynchronously scattered back to the paged KV cache in HBM, and the scatter is issued only when i+1 == Tq (last query block for the sequence).

verification.spec: Run RPA on a small synthetic batch with known new KV; after the kernel, read back the paged KV cache from HBM and assert it equals the expected merged-KV layout for those token positions; also inspect kernel source to confirm the async update is guarded by an `i+1 == Tq` predicate.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that a ragged mixed-batch input generator produces per-sequence (q_len, kv_len) pairs drawn from a configurable distribution with a reproducible seed.

verification.spec: Call the generator with seed=0, n=128, max_tokens=4096, distribution='lognormal' (or 'mixture'); assert (a) sum(q_len) <= max_tokens, (b) len(q_len) == len(kv_len) == n, (c) all kv_len >= q_len, (d) two calls with the same seed produce identical sequences and different seeds produce different sequences.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the distribution-aware segmentation produces indices [i, j, k] partitioning the batch into decode-only (q_len=1), fixed-chunk-prefill, and mixed regions.

verification.spec: Given a batch with explicit q_lens=[1,1,1,128,128,53,77] and a fixed chunk size of 128, call the segmentation function and assert it returns (i=3, j=5, k=7); assert sequences [0,i) all have q_len==1, [i,j) all have q_len==chunk_size, and [j,k) are anything else.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented that (a) pads ragged mixed-batch inputs to fixed upper bounds (max_num_tokens s, max_num_sequences n) so kernel shapes remain static and no JAX recompilation is triggered, and (b) provides a post-scheduling reordering routine that reorders sequences in a mixed batch so that decode-only requests appear first, followed by fixed-size-chunk prefill-only requests, followed by mixed/variable requests, producing the [i, j, k] segmentation indices consumed by the distribution-aware kernel dispatch described in Section 3.4 and Section 3.6.

verification.spec: Import the padding+reordering module from the implementation. Construct synthetic mixed batches with a mix of: decode requests (q_len==1), fixed-chunk prefill requests (q_len==fixed_chunk), and mixed/variable requests (other q_len). Call the reorder function and assert: (1) returned indices (i, j, k) satisfy 0 <= i <= j <= k <= n; (2) all sequences in positions [0, i) have q_len==1; (3) all sequences in positions [i, j) have q_len==fixed_chunk; (4) all remaining sequences are in [j, k); (5) k equals the number of real (non-padding) sequences. Then call the pad function with bounds (s_max, n_max) on a batch whose total tokens < s_max and num_seqs < n_max; assert the returned q tensor has leading token dim == s_max, the per-sequence metadata arrays have length n_max, padding entries are marked (e.g., q_len==0 or a padding mask), and that calling a jit-compiled stub twice with two different real batch sizes (but same bounds) does not retrigger tracing (verify via jax trace counter or jax.jit cache_size()).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented such that the prefill Speed computation in TFLOPs/s follows the paper's two-branch formula contract: a single shared latency input (seconds) and a FLOPs numerator that branches on whether causal masking is applied, returning a value in TFLOPs/s.

verification.spec: Import the prefill Speed/throughput computation function from the implementation. (1) Call it with causal=False, n=1, s=8192, h_q=32, d_k=128, latency_s=1.0 and verify the returned value equals (2 * 2 * n * s * s * h_q * d_k) / 1e12 within 1e-6 relative tolerance. (2) Call it with causal=True and identical other inputs and verify the returned value matches the paper's causal formula (which is NOT simply halved, because each KV compute block still participates in the matmul). (3) Verify that both branches accept the SAME latency argument name/position and return units of TFLOPs/s. (4) Verify that doubling latency_s halves the returned TFLOPs/s in both branches.
